EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated large hail and damaging wind occurred during the evening hours of May 2nd over portions of north central Kansas. A isolated intense thunderstorm developed along the Phillips/Rooks County line around 5PM CDT. This cell remained nearly stationary as it intensified over the next hour and eventually produced large hail around 1 inch in diameter. This storm remained strong to severe over nearly the same area for around 2 hours from 5:30-7:30PM. As this storm weakened entirely by about 8PM, a new line of thunderstorms along a cold front were poised to move in from the W/NW. This line posed a primarily wind threat as it shifted eastward across north central Kansas between 9-11PM. A couple 60-70 MPH wind gusts were reported during this time, but damage was minimal and/or very isolated. The line of thunderstorms weakened considerably by midnight as a developing MCV focused the most intense activity further S across central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 68 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located 1 mile west of Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe weather only briefly clipped the far southeastern fringes of this six-county North Central Kansas  area on this Friday afternoon, but it was nonetheless an active hour or so within south-central Mitchell County, as a short-lived, semi-isolated severe storm entered the local area out of Lincoln County around 4:30 p.m. CDT, peaked in intensity between 5:00-5:30 p.m. CDT and then rapidly dissipated. The only ground-truth report of severe weather came from a CoCoRaHS observer, who noted that severe winds (estimated around 65 MPH) destroyed a farm outbuilding and caused crop damage approximately eight miles south-southwest of Beloit.||This storm first developed/intensified slightly south of the local area over the Russell/Lincoln County border area around 4 p.m. CDT, along a well-defined late summer cold front gradually progressing across the region from west-to-east. Only about 90 minutes later, it quickly weakened over east-central Mitchell County, signaling the end of any severe weather threat within the local area for the remainder of the afternoon-evening, as all further severe activity focused off to both the east and south. In the mid-upper levels, forcing was seasonably-decent, with North Central Kansas receiving a glancing blow from a potent, negatively-tilted shortwave trough swinging northeastward across the Dakotas during the afternoon-evening. However, the aforementioned surface cold front was the main synoptic feature in play, trailing southward through eastern portions of North Central Kansas at mid-afternoon from a deepening 1000-millibar low pressure center over eastern ND. Along and just ahead of this front a very unstable airmass awaited, with the surface temperature/dewpoint in the Mitchell County area at 4 p.m. CDT registering around 94/72 (F). Around this same time, mesoscale parameters featured 3000-4000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 30-40 knots of deep-layer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local CoCoRaHS observer noted that severe winds (estimated around 65 MPH) destroyed a farm outbuilding and caused crop damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent thunderstorms with heavy rain rates led to heavy rainfall and serious flooding in Osborne County during the early morning hours of May 16th. Highest rainfall totals included 8.16 from 1 WNW Codell, mainly between 1-4 AM CDT, 4.19 from 1 S Plainville, 4.11 from 13 SW of Tipton. The axis of heaviest rainfall occurred at the head of and alongside the Paradise Creek, which ultimately led to serious flash flooding of Natoma, Kansas in extreme SW Osborne County. The mayor stated that approximately 120 homes had moderate to severe damage, with another 15 likely a total loss. Approximately 22 businesses had water damage. Many people were displaced and had to be rescued from their homes. Comparisons were made to the flash flood event in 1993.||The upper level pattern consisted of diffluent and active zonal to southwest flow, with strong divergence noted in the right entrance region of 90kt 300mb jet streak running from the northern Plains into the Ohio River Valley. A strong 35-45kt low level jet developed during the late evening of the 15th and continued into the early morning hours of the 16th, with strong convergence noted on the nose of the low level jet over central and northern Kansas. The strong low level convergence, coupled with the strong upper level divergence promoted deep lift through the atmospheric column, amidst precipitable water values that peaked near 1.5���, which caused heavy rain rates. In addition, thunderstorms trained over the Paradise Creek basin particularly frequently, further promoting heavy rainfall amounts.
Large hail was reported during the evening of 10/10 in Coffey County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported brief hail up to golf ball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some weak storms moved through the Kansas City metro, causing some very isolated wind damage in Johnson County. While a tornado was not confirmed, the very isolated and concentrated damage, to go along with the otherwise fairly weak storms lends some suspicion to a brief spin up occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KCTV reported some powerlines down in Olathe. A tornado was not confirmed, but the isolated and concentrated damage, along with otherwise fairly weak convection lends at least some suspicion of a brief and weak circulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Discrete thunderstorms formed along the Colorado Front Range, moving east into northwest Kansas. More discrete storms developed ahead of the Colorado storms along outflow boundaries, eventually forming a linear cluster of storms over a period of two hours as they continued east. These storms produced hail up to quarter in size, wind gusts up to 80 mph, and blowing dust that reduced visibility as low as a few hundred yards at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported via Weather Underground, mesonet station KKSALMEN2 measured a 58 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the multi-day event, multiple bands of light to moderate snowfall rates moved across parts of northwest Kansas. Up to 9.5 inches of snow was reported from these bands, with the highest amounts over Graham County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow over three days produced 7-9 inches of snow across Norton County, Kansas. The greatest amount of snow fell on Sunday night through Monday. The highest total snowfall accumulations were observed near Lenora, Kansas. Key impacts: transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds moved in behind a cold front during the night of the 13th.  These winds caused blowing dust, reducing the visibility down to a mile or so at times.  The lowest visibility with the frontal passage was reported at St. Francis of only a few feet.  As the night progressed the gusts remained strong but decreased below 60 MPH. During the day on the 14th the winds increased to near or above 60 MPH sporadically through the day then declined some that night. On the 15th a few sporadic wind gusts around 60 MPH occurred by early afternoon then declined. Some parts of Northwest Kansas had the high winds end earlier.  The peak wind gust for the event was 77 MPH near Colby.||The dust storm that occurred over far East Central Colorado on the 15th spread into parts of Greeley and Wallace counties.  Visibility fell to zero near Horace.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on proximity there were a couple rounds of wind gusts around 60 MPH during this timeframe.  Blowing dust occurred with the cold front passage, reducing the visibility down to two and a half miles at Norton.  Key impacts: high winds, blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds moved in behind a cold front during the night of the 13th.  These winds caused blowing dust, reducing the visibility down to a mile or so at times.  The lowest visibility with the frontal passage was reported at St. Francis of only a few feet.  As the night progressed the gusts remained strong but decreased below 60 MPH. During the day on the 14th the winds increased to near or above 60 MPH sporadically through the day then declined some that night. On the 15th a few sporadic wind gusts around 60 MPH occurred by early afternoon then declined. Some parts of Northwest Kansas had the high winds end earlier.  The peak wind gust for the event was 77 MPH near Colby.||The dust storm that occurred over far East Central Colorado on the 15th spread into parts of Greeley and Wallace counties.  Visibility fell to zero near Horace.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a couple rounds of wind gusts around 60 MPH during this timeframe.  Blowing dust occurred with the cold front passage, reducing the visibility down to four miles at Hill City.  Key impacts: high winds, blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a roughly 36-hour stretch between the evening of Sunday January 24th and the morning of Tuesday the 26th, a major winter storm took direct aim on the entire NWS Hastings coverage area, dropping a widespread 6-12 of heavy snow, with unofficial higher reports of up to around 15. For all areas, the majority of snow piled up between 6am-6pm on the 25th, snarling travel and shutting down countless schools and businesses at the start of the work-week. For some areas, this was the biggest winter storm (in terms of snow amounts) in at least five years, since at least the Groundhog Day storm/blizzard of Feb. 2, 2016. Although snow amounts varied somewhat across the area (generally highest within counties along/east of the Highway 281 corridor and lowest farther west), this event was a bit unique in that the entire coverage area received several inches of snow, with no truly sharp divide between low amounts and high amounts. While snow was the only precipitation type for most of north central Kansas, a brief period of freezing rain/light icing was noted mainly in/near Mitchell County KS. From a moisture (melted liquid) perspective, this storm was actually very beneficial, featuring widespread totals of mainly 0.50-1.20. This was not a bad thing considering the area was in at least moderate drought going into this storm. Despite causing significant disruptions to travel, fortunately this storm was not a true/crippling blizzard, as sustained winds speeds through most of it averaged 15-25 MPH/gusts up to 30-35 MPH. Snow became dry enough, though, to promote at least moderate drifting towards the end of the event. Temperatures were also not extreme during this event, generally in the teens and 20s. ||Briefly covering the meteorological background and timing: The primary feature was a potent mid-upper level low pressure system that gradually tracked east-northeast from the CO/KS border area on the morning of the 25th, across the heart of our local area during the daylight hours (promoting the heaviest snow during that time), and then departing into IA by daybreak on the 26th. The first flakes started flying on the evening of the 24th, while the last few flurries faded away by sunrise on the 26th. From a forecasting perspective, this storm was a little bit sneaky in the sense that it was not obvious several days beforehand that it would end up being a major storm in terms of snowfall amounts. In fact, as little as 3 days before the event some computer models still suggested a weaker storm, with generally light to moderate amounts. However, by Saturday afternoon (Jan. 23), concern had grown enough in the potential for a bigger storm that the first Winter Storm Watch was issued for southeastern portions of the area. By early Sunday morning (Jan. 24), it was finally very apparent that this system was indeed going to be a major snow-maker, and a Winter Storm Warning was issued for all of north central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two-day storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 9 inches across the county. The highest totals included 7.5 inches from a CoCoRaHS observer located 5 E of Phillipsburg and 6.0 inches from the Cooperative Observer located in Logan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure system brings prolonged period of strong to severe wind gusts to north central Kansas from the early morning hours on the 14th, though the afternoon on the 15th. The initial surge of strong wind gusts of 50-60mph occurred along and behind of a strong cold front that was associated with an intense (sub-990 mb) surface low pressure near the North Dakota/Canada border. This initial surge of northwesterly wind gusts arrived during the early morning hours of January 13th, between midnight and 2am. Wind gusts weakened for a period early Thursday morning before increasing once again during the mid to late morning as the primary surface low dropped southeast from northern MN into northeast IA. The strong winds were the result of steep low level lapse rates and efficient mixing into an intense low level jet. ||Wind gusts up to 65mph are not terribly unusual for this area, but what set this system apart from many others was the long duration of strong winds - around 24 to 36 hours! In fact, despite many areas reaching High Wind Warning criteria on Thursday, March 14, many areas of north central Kansas actually experienced their peak wind gusts from this event during the morning hours of the 15th. Damage and impacts from the high wind were fairly typical, generally ranging from tree and power line damage to some roof damage. There were no known widespread or long-lasting power outages from this event at the time of this writing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent gusts of 55 to 65 MPH were reported across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Over the course of a roughly 48-hour period between the mid-late morning of Friday the 12th and the mid-late morning of Sunday the 14th, this six-county North Central Kansas area saw the invasion of two separate snow events in fairly rapid succession. Cumulative amounts of the fairly dry, fluffy flakes varied somewhat, from as little as 1.5 to 3 inches for the majority of Jewell, Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties, to as much as 4-6 (localized higher) for especially the northern halves of Phillips and Smith counties. Per several NWS cooperative observers and CoCoRaHS, some of the highest 48-hour totals featured: 6.4 in Phillipsburg, 6.0 six miles east of Phillipsburg, 5.0 at Logan and 4.8 in Lebanon. Winds were not much of a factor during either round, given speeds commonly 5-15 MPH and only occasional gusts over 20 MPH. These light winds were promoted by stout and very cold Arctic high pressure centered over the Northern Plains, but which dominated the weather situation southward throughout the entire central United States. In fact, the anomalously cold temperatures gripping North Central Kansas between the 12th-14th were likely a bigger story than the snow, as daytime highs only reached the single digits above zero (F) each day. ||Taking a closer look at the meteorological background/timing of this multi-round event, while each of the two main pulses of snow were driven primarily by mid-level frontogenesis, the first round on the 12th-13th occurred under predominantly zonal flow aloft, while the second round on the 13th-14th unfolded under more southwesterly flow as a broad trough evolved over the southwestern United States. Focusing solely on Round 1, this actually consisted of a few mini-rounds of snow, one mainly during the daytime on the 12th and the next sliding through within a few hours before sunrise on the 13th. The highest official total from Round 1 was 3.2 in Phillipsburg. Then, following a roughly 12-hour break in the action (including quite a bit of sunshine during the day on the 13th), Round 2 arrived during the evening, persisted through the night and mostly-ended by noon on the 14th. The highest Round 2 total was 3.2 in both Phillipsburg and Lebanon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cumulative snow totals from two main rounds within a 54-hour period included: 3.0 inches at Beloit and 2.0 inches in Cawker City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a historically cold Arctic air mass in place, snow overspread the region on Valentine's Day and continued through February 15th. Most locations across southern and eastern Kansas picked up between 2 and 6 inches of snow. Because it was so cold, snow was very light and fluffy and was also easy to blow into drifts. A few totals even reached a depth of 8 inches and some drifts of snow were reported to be around 9 inches deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several snow reports across the county of 4 to 6 inches were relayed to the NWS.  The highest was 6 inches at Wellington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-February 2021 unleashed a relentless blast of brutal cold, the likes of which North Central Kansas had not endured in several decades. Of the few long-time NWS cooperative observer temperature stations within the six-county area, the 10-day stretch from the 7th-16th marked the overall-coldest 10 days in either 32 years (since Dec. 1989) or 38 years (since Dec. 1983)...based on average/mean temperature. Focusing specifically on the Smith Center observer data (84 years of available records), the 10-day average temperature between the 7th-16th was -0.3 degrees (F), an incredible 32 degrees below the 30-year normal. Daily highs over the course of the 10 days ranged from -2 to +14 degrees, while daily lows ranged from -20 to 0. Among other temperature tidbits for Smith Center, nine straight days of sub-zero lows at the official NWS observer station matched the previous record first achieved in Dec. 1983, and the nearby unofficial airport AWOS spent AROUND 45 HOURS AT-OR-BELOW ZERO between the early afternoon of the 14th and late morning of the 16th (this likely hadn't occurred since at least Feb. 1989). In addition, the low temperature of -20 on the 15th set a new February record (coldest low since Dec. 1989), while the high temperature of -2 on the 15th matched the existing February record first set in 1989. Factoring in winds (albeit relatively light through the majority of the 10-day period), the most frigid wind chill readings occurred between the evening of the 14th and the morning of the 16th, prompting a long-fuse Wind Chill Warning for all of North Central Kansas. On both mornings of the 15th and 16th, a handful of unofficial automated stations recorded wind chill values well down into the -30 to -40 range, highlighted by -37 at mesonet stations near Lebanon and Kirwin on the 15th and -37 at Smith Center airport on the 16th. ||Focusing on impacts, the local North Central Kansas area was just a tiny sliver of an expansive swath of the central and southern United States that battled this historic cold, as sub-freezing temperatures surged all the way into south Texas (even promoting a light coating of snow at Galveston). This cold outbreak put a considerable strain on not only the local, but especially the regional power and energy industry. While North Central Kansas avoided more serious/longer-lasting power disruptions that ravaged many southern states, fairly brief rolling blackouts (typically around 30 minutes) occurred within the area on the 15th-16th, as called for by the Southwest Power Pool (the independent regional transmission organization that manages the electric grid and wholesale power market for portions of 14 central and southern states, including Kansas). Turning to the large-scale meteorological picture, this prolonged/intense cold outbreak was driven in the mid-upper levels by a parade of troughs circulating around a quasi-stationary parent vortex churning over southern Canada, each one further reinforcing/intensifying the magnitude of Arctic air allowed to spill southward through the central United States. At the surface, persistent, predominantly northerly flow prevailed for days on end as a sprawling Arctic high increasingly-dominated much of the central and northeastern United States. Also contributing to the magnitude of persistent cold in North Central Kansas was a variable blanket of snow cover, generally ranging from 1-2 inches south of Highway 24 to as deep as 6-9 inches in some areas along/north of Highway 36, where a series of snow events brushed the area between the 6th-14th. Concluding with a brief rundown of how North Central Kansas finally crawled out from underneath the 10-day crush of ruthless cold, improvement finally got underway on the 17th and trended upward for several days thereafter, with high temperatures across most of the area recovering into the teens on the 17th, 20s on the 18th, 30s-40s on the 19th (ending a 13-day sub-freezing streak at Smith Center), 40s-50s on the 20th, and even reaching the 70s by the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The following are the coldest recorded wind chills for the period: on the morning of Feb. 15th: -37 degrees two miles south of Lebanon; -35 degrees at Smith Center Airport AWOS. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds moved in behind a cold front during the night of the 13th.  These winds caused blowing dust, reducing the visibility down to a mile or so at times.  The lowest visibility with the frontal passage was reported at St. Francis of only a few feet.  As the night progressed the gusts remained strong but decreased below 60 MPH. During the day on the 14th the winds increased to near or above 60 MPH sporadically through the day then declined some that night. On the 15th a few sporadic wind gusts around 60 MPH occurred by early afternoon then declined. Some parts of Northwest Kansas had the high winds end earlier.  The peak wind gust for the event was 77 MPH near Colby.||The dust storm that occurred over far East Central Colorado on the 15th spread into parts of Greeley and Wallace counties.  Visibility fell to zero near Horace.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several rounds of wind gusts around 60 MPH during this timeframe.  Key impacts: high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure system brings prolonged period of strong to severe wind gusts to north central Kansas from the early morning hours on the 14th, though the afternoon on the 15th. The initial surge of strong wind gusts of 50-60mph occurred along and behind of a strong cold front that was associated with an intense (sub-990 mb) surface low pressure near the North Dakota/Canada border. This initial surge of northwesterly wind gusts arrived during the early morning hours of January 13th, between midnight and 2am. Wind gusts weakened for a period early Thursday morning before increasing once again during the mid to late morning as the primary surface low dropped southeast from northern MN into northeast IA. The strong winds were the result of steep low level lapse rates and efficient mixing into an intense low level jet. ||Wind gusts up to 65mph are not terribly unusual for this area, but what set this system apart from many others was the long duration of strong winds - around 24 to 36 hours! In fact, despite many areas reaching High Wind Warning criteria on Thursday, March 14, many areas of north central Kansas actually experienced their peak wind gusts from this event during the morning hours of the 15th. Damage and impacts from the high wind were fairly typical, generally ranging from tree and power line damage to some roof damage. There were no known widespread or long-lasting power outages from this event at the time of this writing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent gusts of 55 to 65 MPH were reported across the region. The peak measured gust in the county was 60 MPH, recorded at 2:14 PM CST on January 15th by a mesonet located near Randall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During a roughly 36-hour stretch between the evening of Sunday January 24th and the morning of Tuesday the 26th, a major winter storm took direct aim on the entire NWS Hastings coverage area, dropping a widespread 6-12 of heavy snow, with unofficial higher reports of up to around 15. For all areas, the majority of snow piled up between 6am-6pm on the 25th, snarling travel and shutting down countless schools and businesses at the start of the work-week. For some areas, this was the biggest winter storm (in terms of snow amounts) in at least five years, since at least the Groundhog Day storm/blizzard of Feb. 2, 2016. Although snow amounts varied somewhat across the area (generally highest within counties along/east of the Highway 281 corridor and lowest farther west), this event was a bit unique in that the entire coverage area received several inches of snow, with no truly sharp divide between low amounts and high amounts. While snow was the only precipitation type for most of north central Kansas, a brief period of freezing rain/light icing was noted mainly in/near Mitchell County KS. From a moisture (melted liquid) perspective, this storm was actually very beneficial, featuring widespread totals of mainly 0.50-1.20. This was not a bad thing considering the area was in at least moderate drought going into this storm. Despite causing significant disruptions to travel, fortunately this storm was not a true/crippling blizzard, as sustained winds speeds through most of it averaged 15-25 MPH/gusts up to 30-35 MPH. Snow became dry enough, though, to promote at least moderate drifting towards the end of the event. Temperatures were also not extreme during this event, generally in the teens and 20s. ||Briefly covering the meteorological background and timing: The primary feature was a potent mid-upper level low pressure system that gradually tracked east-northeast from the CO/KS border area on the morning of the 25th, across the heart of our local area during the daylight hours (promoting the heaviest snow during that time), and then departing into IA by daybreak on the 26th. The first flakes started flying on the evening of the 24th, while the last few flurries faded away by sunrise on the 26th. From a forecasting perspective, this storm was a little bit sneaky in the sense that it was not obvious several days beforehand that it would end up being a major storm in terms of snowfall amounts. In fact, as little as 3 days before the event some computer models still suggested a weaker storm, with generally light to moderate amounts. However, by Saturday afternoon (Jan. 23), concern had grown enough in the potential for a bigger storm that the first Winter Storm Watch was issued for southeastern portions of the area. By early Sunday morning (Jan. 24), it was finally very apparent that this system was indeed going to be a major snow-maker, and a Winter Storm Warning was issued for all of north central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two-day storm total snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches across the county. The highest totals included 12.0 inches from a CoCoRaHS observer located 2 ENE of Webber, 8.8 inches from the Cooperative Observer in Ionia, and 6.0 inches from the Cooperative Observer in Jewell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a historically cold Arctic air mass in place, snow overspread the region on Valentine's Day and continued through February 15th. Most locations across southern and eastern Kansas picked up between 2 and 6 inches of snow. Because it was so cold, snow was very light and fluffy and was also easy to blow into drifts. A few totals even reached a depth of 8 inches and some drifts of snow were reported to be around 9 inches deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer in Arkansas City reported 6 inches of snow.  Many other locations across the county ranged from 4 to 6 inches in depth.

A few totals even reached a depth of 8 inches and some drifts of snow were reported to be around 9 inches deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most locations reported up to 5 inches of snow with this event.  However, an additional 4 to 5 inch snowdrifts were noted along the roads bringing the depth up to 9 or 10 inches along with treacherous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic cold outbreak overspread all of the Plains on February 6th and lasted through February 18th. A persistent Arctic airmass like this hasn't affected the region since the 1980s. There were days where the high temperature did not make it out of the single digits. The Arctic surge made it all the way down to south Texas, where snow was observed on the beaches of Galveston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant outbreak of cold weather occurred across all of central and southeast Kansas.  Numerous records were broken, tied, or were very close.  These included the consecutive number of days below 32 degrees, 20 degrees, or overnight lows below 0 degrees. Widespread pipe failures were noted across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-February 2021 unleashed a relentless blast of brutal cold, the likes of which North Central Kansas had not endured in several decades. Of the few long-time NWS cooperative observer temperature stations within the six-county area, the 10-day stretch from the 7th-16th marked the overall-coldest 10 days in either 32 years (since Dec. 1989) or 38 years (since Dec. 1983)...based on average/mean temperature. Focusing specifically on the Smith Center observer data (84 years of available records), the 10-day average temperature between the 7th-16th was -0.3 degrees (F), an incredible 32 degrees below the 30-year normal. Daily highs over the course of the 10 days ranged from -2 to +14 degrees, while daily lows ranged from -20 to 0. Among other temperature tidbits for Smith Center, nine straight days of sub-zero lows at the official NWS observer station matched the previous record first achieved in Dec. 1983, and the nearby unofficial airport AWOS spent AROUND 45 HOURS AT-OR-BELOW ZERO between the early afternoon of the 14th and late morning of the 16th (this likely hadn't occurred since at least Feb. 1989). In addition, the low temperature of -20 on the 15th set a new February record (coldest low since Dec. 1989), while the high temperature of -2 on the 15th matched the existing February record first set in 1989. Factoring in winds (albeit relatively light through the majority of the 10-day period), the most frigid wind chill readings occurred between the evening of the 14th and the morning of the 16th, prompting a long-fuse Wind Chill Warning for all of North Central Kansas. On both mornings of the 15th and 16th, a handful of unofficial automated stations recorded wind chill values well down into the -30 to -40 range, highlighted by -37 at mesonet stations near Lebanon and Kirwin on the 15th and -37 at Smith Center airport on the 16th. ||Focusing on impacts, the local North Central Kansas area was just a tiny sliver of an expansive swath of the central and southern United States that battled this historic cold, as sub-freezing temperatures surged all the way into south Texas (even promoting a light coating of snow at Galveston). This cold outbreak put a considerable strain on not only the local, but especially the regional power and energy industry. While North Central Kansas avoided more serious/longer-lasting power disruptions that ravaged many southern states, fairly brief rolling blackouts (typically around 30 minutes) occurred within the area on the 15th-16th, as called for by the Southwest Power Pool (the independent regional transmission organization that manages the electric grid and wholesale power market for portions of 14 central and southern states, including Kansas). Turning to the large-scale meteorological picture, this prolonged/intense cold outbreak was driven in the mid-upper levels by a parade of troughs circulating around a quasi-stationary parent vortex churning over southern Canada, each one further reinforcing/intensifying the magnitude of Arctic air allowed to spill southward through the central United States. At the surface, persistent, predominantly northerly flow prevailed for days on end as a sprawling Arctic high increasingly-dominated much of the central and northeastern United States. Also contributing to the magnitude of persistent cold in North Central Kansas was a variable blanket of snow cover, generally ranging from 1-2 inches south of Highway 24 to as deep as 6-9 inches in some areas along/north of Highway 36, where a series of snow events brushed the area between the 6th-14th. Concluding with a brief rundown of how North Central Kansas finally crawled out from underneath the 10-day crush of ruthless cold, improvement finally got underway on the 17th and trended upward for several days thereafter, with high temperatures across most of the area recovering into the teens on the 17th, 20s on the 18th, 30s-40s on the 19th (ending a 13-day sub-freezing streak at Smith Center), 40s-50s on the 20th, and even reaching the 70s by the 23rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The following are the coldest recorded wind chills for the period: on the morning of Feb. 15th: -37 degrees four miles west-southwest of Kirwin; -35 degrees six miles east of Phillipsburg; -34 degrees two miles north of Phillipsburg; -33 degrees one mile northeast of Woodruff. On the morning of Feb. 16th: -35 degrees one mile west of Logan; -34 degrees six miles east of Phillipsburg; -32 degrees two miles north of Phillipsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic cold outbreak overspread all of the Plains on February 6th and lasted through February 18th. A persistent Arctic airmass like this hasn't affected the region since the 1980s. There were days where the high temperature did not make it out of the single digits. The Arctic surge made it all the way down to south Texas, where snow was observed on the beaches of Galveston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant outbreak of cold weather occurred across all of central and southeast Kansas. Numerous records were broken, tied, or were very close. These included the consecutive number of days below 32 degrees, 20 degrees, or overnight lows below 0 degrees. Widespread pipe failures were noted across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed along a dry line northeast of Dodge City and moved northeast into central Kansas during the evening hours of April 6th, 2021. The hail ranged in size from quarter to half dollar size with a few 60 mph wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail occurred along Canyon Road and ranged in size from nickel to quarter size.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Fire department reported the gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed along a dry line northeast of Dodge City and moved northeast into central Kansas during the evening hours of April 6th, 2021. The hail ranged in size from quarter to half dollar size with a few 60 mph wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was slightly larger than quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season cold snap brought freezing temperatures to southeast Kansas. With many fruit trees in the flowering process and the winter wheat crop beginning to grow, widespread prolonged temperatures well below freezing in the  20s impacted these crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread low temperatures between 28 and 30 degrees were reported around the county the morning of April 21, 2021. Arma reported 28 degrees, while Frontenac reported a low of 30 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary slowly pushed north across eastern Colorado during the afternoon hours before shifting east into western Kansas and extreme southwest Nebraska during the evening hours. As the storms moved out of Colorado, they evolved into a line with embedded supercell thunderstorms that moved across northwest Kansas. The line of  storms produced up to tea cup sized hail and up to 70 mph wind gusts. Damage to evergreen tree limbs and a roof occurred on a property along the Colorado/Kansas state line in Cheyenne County due to large hail and thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong embedded supercell thunderstorm produced tea cup sized hail as it crossed the Colorado/Kansas state line. The large hail damaged the roof of a home at the location of the report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong embedded supercell thunderstorm produced strong winds estimated at 60-70 mph as it crossed the Colorado/Kansas state line. The strong winds were able to snap off a few tree limbs of an unknown size off evergreen trees at the location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the multi-day event, multiple bands of light to moderate snowfall rates moved across parts of northwest Kansas. Up to 9.5 inches of snow was reported from these bands, with the highest amounts over Graham County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow over three days produced up to 6 inches of snow across Sherman County, Kansas. The greatest amount of snow fell on Sunday night through Monday. The highest total snowfall accumulations were observed near Goodland, Kansas. Key impacts: transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High winds moved in behind a cold front during the night of the 13th.  These winds caused blowing dust, reducing the visibility down to a mile or so at times.  The lowest visibility with the frontal passage was reported at St. Francis of only a few feet.  As the night progressed the gusts remained strong but decreased below 60 MPH. During the day on the 14th the winds increased to near or above 60 MPH sporadically through the day then declined some that night. On the 15th a few sporadic wind gusts around 60 MPH occurred by early afternoon then declined. Some parts of Northwest Kansas had the high winds end earlier.  The peak wind gust for the event was 77 MPH near Colby.||The dust storm that occurred over far East Central Colorado on the 15th spread into parts of Greeley and Wallace counties.  Visibility fell to zero near Horace.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several rounds of wind gusts around 60 MPH during this timeframe.  Blowing dust was reported as the cold front moved through, which reduced the visibility to a mile and a half.  Key impacts: high winds, blowing dust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was one round of wind gusts around 60 MPH during this timeframe.  Key impacts: high winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air moved into the High Plains following a winter weather system that brought up to 9.5 inches of snow to northwest Kansas over a span of three days. West-southwesterly to northwesterly winds sustained up to 8 mph caused wind chills as low as 22 degrees below zero to occur during the early morning hours. The cold temperatures caused sporadic power outages across northwest Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwest to west-southwest winds up to 3 mph combined with cold temperatures as cold as 11 below produced wind chills as low as 21 degrees below zero across Wallace. The coldest wind chills were observed near Weskan, Kansas. Impacts: power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface disturbance moved across the High Plains initiating the development of isolated thunderstorms during the afternoon hours. These storms produced strong thunderstorm wind gusts up to 68 mph and were able to blow over a semi truck traveling on I-70.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As isolated storms moved east along the I-70 corridor, outflow associated with the storms produced strong winds up to 60 mph which blew over an empty semi truck traveling on I-70 near mile marker 61. Key Impacts: strong winds, difficult driving conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure system brings prolonged period of strong to severe wind gusts to north central Kansas from the early morning hours on the 14th, though the afternoon on the 15th. The initial surge of strong wind gusts of 50-60mph occurred along and behind of a strong cold front that was associated with an intense (sub-990 mb) surface low pressure near the North Dakota/Canada border. This initial surge of northwesterly wind gusts arrived during the early morning hours of January 13th, between midnight and 2am. Wind gusts weakened for a period early Thursday morning before increasing once again during the mid to late morning as the primary surface low dropped southeast from northern MN into northeast IA. The strong winds were the result of steep low level lapse rates and efficient mixing into an intense low level jet. ||Wind gusts up to 65mph are not terribly unusual for this area, but what set this system apart from many others was the long duration of strong winds - around 24 to 36 hours! In fact, despite many areas reaching High Wind Warning criteria on Thursday, March 14, many areas of north central Kansas actually experienced their peak wind gusts from this event during the morning hours of the 15th. Damage and impacts from the high wind were fairly typical, generally ranging from tree and power line damage to some roof damage. There were no known widespread or long-lasting power outages from this event at the time of this writing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent gusts of 55 to 65 MPH were reported across the region. The peak measured gust in the county was 62 MPH, recorded at 11:19 AM CST on January 15th by a mesonet located near Hunter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the multi-day event, multiple bands of light to moderate snowfall rates moved across parts of northwest Kansas. Up to 9.5 inches of snow was reported from these bands, with the highest amounts over Graham County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple bands of snow over three days produced 3.5-7.7 inches of snow across Logan County, Kansas. The greatest amount of snow fell on Sunday night through Monday. The highest total snowfall accumulations were observed near Russell Springs, Kansas. Key impacts: transportation delays.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air moved into the High Plains following a winter weather system that brought up to 9.5 inches of snow to northwest Kansas over a span of three days. West-southwesterly to northwesterly winds sustained up to 8 mph caused wind chills as low as 22 degrees below zero to occur during the early morning hours. The cold temperatures caused sporadic power outages across northwest Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West to northwesterly winds up to 6 mph combined with cold temperatures as cold as 10 below produced wind chills as low as 22 degrees below zero across Greeley County. The coldest wind chills were observed near Tribune, Kansas. Impacts: power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic cold outbreak overspread all of the Plains on February 6th and lasted through February 18th. A persistent Arctic airmass like this hasn't affected the region since the 1980s. There were days where the high temperature did not make it out of the single digits. The Arctic surge made it all the way down to south Texas, where snow was observed on the beaches of Galveston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant outbreak of cold weather occurred across all of central and southeast Kansas. Numerous records were broken, tied, or were very close. These included the consecutive number of days below 32 degrees, 20 degrees, or overnight lows below 0 degrees. Widespread pipe failures were noted across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a historically cold Arctic air mass in place, snow overspread the region on Valentine's Day and continued through February 15th. Most locations across southern and eastern Kansas picked up between 2 and 6 inches of snow. Because it was so cold, snow was very light and fluffy and was also easy to blow into drifts. A few totals even reached a depth of 8 inches and some drifts of snow were reported to be around 9 inches deep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most locations reported up to 5 inches of snow with this event.  However, an additional 4 snowdrifts were noted along the roads bringing the depth up to 9 inches along with treacherous travel conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms developed along a dry line northeast of Dodge City and moved northeast into central Kansas during the evening hours of April 6th, 2021. The hail ranged in size from quarter to half dollar size with a few 60 mph wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail occurred at the dispatch center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season cold snap brought freezing temperatures to southeast Kansas. With many fruit trees in the flowering process and the winter wheat crop beginning to grow, widespread prolonged temperatures well below freezing in the  20s impacted these crops.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread low temperatures between 28 and 31 degrees were reported around the county the morning of April 21, 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary slowly pushed north across eastern Colorado during the afternoon hours before shifting east into western Kansas and extreme southwest Nebraska during the evening hours. As the storms moved out of Colorado, they evolved into a line with embedded supercell thunderstorms that moved across northwest Kansas. The line of  storms produced up to tea cup sized hail and up to 70 mph wind gusts. Damage to evergreen tree limbs and a roof occurred on a property along the Colorado/Kansas state line in Cheyenne County due to large hail and thunderstorm wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An embedded supercell thunderstorm produced large hail varying in size from quarters to ping pong ball in size. Most of the hailstones were quarter in size or smaller with a few ping pong balls mixed in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For at least part of February, portions of two of the six North Central Kansas counties (Phillips/Smith) contained either severe (D2) or Extreme (D3) Drought per the U.S. Drought Monitor. This marked the fourth-straight month with at least D2 assigned to some part of the area. However, the local coverage of D2/D3 actually shrunk during February, as D2 was removed at mid-month from northwestern Smith County and also most of Phillips County, while the sliver of D3 in far northwestern Phillips County was also trimmed away. As a result, by month's end only a small section of northwestern Phillips County remained in D2. Meanwhile, the majority of the six-county area hung onto Moderate Drought (D1) throughout the month, including all of Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties. On the most positive note, much of Jewell County actually improved to Abnormally Dry (D0). ||The fact that this entire North Central Kansas area either saw a one-category improvement or maintained status quo throughout February was actually a bit surprising, considering that at least 80 percent of it was drier than normal...in fact well-below normal especially within Rooks, Osborne and Mitchell counties. The limited area of slightly above normal precipitation was confined to northern Phillips, Smith and Jewell counties, which was brushed by the far southern edge of several early-to-mid-month snow events that primarily targeted adjacent Nebraska. Overall though, the majority of the local area only measured 0.10-0.40 of February precipitation (generally 15-55 percent of normal). Per several NWS cooperative observers, a few of the higher/lower extremes included: 0.67 at Lovewell Dam and 0.45 in Lebanon...versus a measly 0.06 four miles west-northwest of Plainville and 0.08 at Covert. Temperature-wise, February was bitterly-cold overall, with most of North Central Kansas averaging 10-12 degrees (F) below normal, good for a top-five-coldest on record at most official NWS stations. In fact, the 10-day stretch from the 7th-16th constituted the overall-coldest 10 days in 32-38 years across much of the area (since either December 1989 or 1983).

High winds moved in behind a cold front during the night of the 13th.  These winds caused blowing dust, reducing the visibility down to a mile or so at times.  The lowest visibility with the frontal passage was reported at St. Francis of only a few feet.  As the night progressed the gusts remained strong but decreased below 60 MPH. During the day on the 14th the winds increased to near or above 60 MPH sporadically through the day then declined some that night. On the 15th a few sporadic wind gusts around 60 MPH occurred by early afternoon then declined. Some parts of Northwest Kansas had the high winds end earlier.  The peak wind gust for the event was 77 MPH near Colby.||The dust storm that occurred over far East Central Colorado on the 15th spread into parts of Greeley and Wallace counties.  Visibility fell to zero near Horace.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several rounds of wind gusts around 60 MPH during this timeframe.  Blowing dust also occurred with the cold front passage, dropping the visibility down to a mile and a quarter in Oberlin.  Key impacts: high winds, blowing dust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure system brings prolonged period of strong to severe wind gusts to north central Kansas from the early morning hours on the 14th, though the afternoon on the 15th. The initial surge of strong wind gusts of 50-60mph occurred along and behind of a strong cold front that was associated with an intense (sub-990 mb) surface low pressure near the North Dakota/Canada border. This initial surge of northwesterly wind gusts arrived during the early morning hours of January 13th, between midnight and 2am. Wind gusts weakened for a period early Thursday morning before increasing once again during the mid to late morning as the primary surface low dropped southeast from northern MN into northeast IA. The strong winds were the result of steep low level lapse rates and efficient mixing into an intense low level jet. ||Wind gusts up to 65mph are not terribly unusual for this area, but what set this system apart from many others was the long duration of strong winds - around 24 to 36 hours! In fact, despite many areas reaching High Wind Warning criteria on Thursday, March 14, many areas of north central Kansas actually experienced their peak wind gusts from this event during the morning hours of the 15th. Damage and impacts from the high wind were fairly typical, generally ranging from tree and power line damage to some roof damage. There were no known widespread or long-lasting power outages from this event at the time of this writing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent gusts of 55 to 65 MPH were reported across the region.  The peak measured gust in the county was 65 MPH, recorded at 11:54 AM CST on January 15th by a mesonet located 5 SSW of Stockton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For at least most of January, portions of three of the six North Central Kansas counties (Phillips/Smith/Jewell) were assigned either Severe (D2) or Extreme (D3) Drought by the U.S. Drought Monitor. Although this marked the third consecutive month with at least D2 assigned to some part of the area, overall January featured positive news for drought trends as essentially all of North Central Kansas received above normal precipitation (for particularly most of Phillips County this ended a five-month run of below normal). Despite the encouraging precipitation trends, official drought categories only underwent fairly minimal change during January, although there was a slight reduction in the coverage of D2 and a modest increase of D1 (Moderate Drought). More specifically, the majority of the six-county area spent most of the month in D1, as those portions of Jewell, Mitchell, eastern Osborne and southeastern Smith counties that had recently improved to D0 (Abnormally Dry) actually reverted back to D1 in mid-January. Meanwhile, D2 remained intact across much of northern Phillips and northwestern Smith counties all month, but was removed from northeastern Smith and extreme northwestern Jewell counties on the 26th. Finally, D3 (Extreme Drought) lingered on within extreme northwestern Phillips County. The late-month shrinkage of the D2 in Smith/Jewell counties was driven by a potent winter storm that dumped 5-10 inches of snow across most of North Central Kansas, primarily on the 25th. In fact, this single storm accounted for around 80 percent of total January precipitation, with monthly totals across most of the six-county area ranging from 0.70-1.00 (generally 125-180 percent of normal). Per a few dozen NWS cooperative observer/CoCoRaHS stations, a few of the highest monthly totals featured: 1.43 at Lovewell Dam and 1.29 in Cawker City, with a few of the smallest (but still above normal) tallies including 0.68 at Natoma and 0.69 in Logan. Temperature-wise, although certainly not as big of a drought-factor as it would have been during the growing season, January marked the third-straight warmer-than-average month, with most local stations averaging 3-5 degrees (F) above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same surface boundary from the day before was draped further north with a dry line extending to the south near the Hwy 83 corridor kicking off initial supercells mid-afternoon. As the storms progressed east and northeast, they formed more of a complex line of storms with more of a wind threat rather than the initial hail threat. The thunderstorms moved east out of the CWA near sunset. Behind the front, strong cold air advection allowed for gusty north wind ranging from 55-60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong wind behind a cold front at the Dodge City Regional airport.

A cluster of thunderstorms, which included supercells, developed along a line from Plainville to Smith Center between 6-7PM CDT and gradually shifted E through the evening. The strongest activity affected Smith County where large hail up to 2��� in diameter was reported. Severe thunderstorms pushed east of the area by 9PM CDT, but non-severe thunderstorms continued until almost midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to half dollar size hail was reported.

An arctic air mass moved northwestern Kansas and resided in the area for several days. Wind chills warmed during the daytime before dropping to or below Wind Chill Warning criteria during the night. Area overnight wind chills dropped below -25 degrees Fahrenheit with -45 degrees Fahrenheit being the minimum wind chill recorded during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight variable sustained winds with speeds ranging up to 3 to 7 mph combined with overnight minimum temperatures as cold as -20 degrees Fahrenheit produced wind chills as low as -35 degrees Fahrenheit across Graham County. The coldest wind chills were observed at the ASOS at Hill City, Kansas during the morning of February 15th. Impacts: extreme cold.

Area overnight wind chills dropped below -25 degrees Fahrenheit with -45 degrees Fahrenheit being the minimum wind chill recorded during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight variable sustained winds with speeds ranging up to 3 to 15 mph combined with overnight minimum temperatures as cold as -25 degrees Fahrenheit produced wind chills as low as -41 degrees Fahrenheit across Thomas County. The coldest wind chills were observed at the AWOS at Colby, Kansas during the morning of February 15th. Impacts: extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Discrete thunderstorms formed along the Colorado Front Range, moving east into northwest Kansas. More discrete storms developed ahead of the Colorado storms along outflow boundaries, eventually forming a linear cluster of storms over a period of two hours as they continued east. These storms produced hail up to quarter in size, wind gusts up to 80 mph, and blowing dust that reduced visibility as low as a few hundred yards at times.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: ASOS station KHLC measured a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic air mass moved northwestern Kansas and resided in the area for several days. Wind chills warmed during the daytime before dropping to or below Wind Chill Warning criteria during the night. Area overnight wind chills dropped below -25 degrees Fahrenheit with -45 degrees Fahrenheit being the minimum wind chill recorded during this time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Overnight variable sustained winds with speeds ranging up to 3 to 23 mph combined with overnight minimum temperatures as cold as -24 degrees Fahrenheit produced wind chills as low as -39 degrees Fahrenheit across Sherman County. The coldest wind chills were observed thirteen miles north-northwest of Ruleton, Kansas during the morning of February 15th. Impacts: extreme cold.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same surface boundary from the day before was draped further north with a dry line extending to the south near the Hwy 83 corridor kicking off initial supercells mid-afternoon. The thunderstorms moved east out of the CWA near sunset. Behind the front, strong cold air advection allowed for gusty north wind ranging from 55-60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although it did not cause significant travel issues, and it only stuck around briefly before rapidly melting, much of this six-county north central Kansas area accumulated one of the heaviest snows on record to occur this late in the spring. Specifically, the majority of the area measured somewhere between 1.5 and 5.5 inches of wet, slushy snow between the late evening of Monday the 19th and sunrise on Tuesday the 20th. Per CoCoRaHS/NWS observer measurements and NOHRSC analysis, the highest totals of 4-6 targeted the southern-most local counties (Rooks/Osborne/Mitchell), including 6 near Hunter, 5.5 in Natoma and Covert, 5 ten miles south-southwest of Beloit, and 4.5 four miles west-northwest of Plainville. Within the northern three counties (Phillips/Smith/Jewell) a few of the higher tallies included 3 at Ionia and 2.6 in Lebanon. A sampling of NWS cooperative observer stations for which this was the heaviest snow on record to occur on/after April 20th included: Natoma, Smith Center, Plainville 4WNW, Cawker City and Covert. ||As mentioned, this snow did not stick around long, as while it accumulated during overnight temperatures in the upper 20s-low 30s (F), it melted quickly under widespread sunshine on the 20th as afternoon readings climbed well into the 40s. In the mid-upper levels, this event was driven by the gradual north-to-south passage of a fairly narrow, but intense zone of mid-level frontogenesis (very evident at 700 millibars). In the bigger picture aloft, this frontogenetic zone intensified along the southern edges of an expansive trough dropping south-southeast through the central United States. At the surface, winds across North Central Kansas during this event were  not a major issue, averaging only 5-15 MPH from the north. These relatively light breezes were fostered by expansive high pressure (1030 millibars over the Northern Rockies) oozing southeastward through much of the central and northwestern United States, in the wake of a strong cold front that surged southward across North Central Kansas about 24 hours prior to this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts of 1 to 3 inches were reported across the county, including 2 at Kirwin Dam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front passing through northwestern Kansas caused sustained winds between 20 and 25 mph with gusts up to 40 mph near Hill City, KS. These winds helped drive a wildfire that broke out during the afternoon hours. A residential home was damaged and a few outbuildings destroyed. The fire reportedly spread across 1300 acres of CRP land.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County dispatch reported a wildfire started by a bailer located 5 miles SW of Hill City, KS on the afternoon of April 11. The fire was wind-driven on CRP land and involved 1300 acres. A residential house was damaged and a few outbuildings were destroyed. The house required kitchen to be rebuilt along with some work done to the attic. End time for event is radar estimated. Nearby the Hill City ASOS reported NNW winds at 20 to 25 mph with gusts up to 40 mph along relative humidity values around 21% to 23% during this time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Throughout the day, a cold front moved south across Northwest Kansas. During the morning hours, a line of showers and thunderstorms developed along the front. As the storms moved southeast, a few storms became isolated towards the tail end of the front. One of the isolated storms strengthened over Graham County, producing pea to nickel sized hail south of Hill City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to nickel sized hail observed as storm moved over the location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected portions of north central Kansas during the evening hours of May 26th. Initial thunderstorm development for this event occurred well west of the area across NW Kansas and SW Nebraska during the late afternoon hours, in which several supercells developed in the vicinity of a low pressure, warm front, and dry line triple point. This is where the most severe weather, including tornadoes, occurred. Additional intense supercells developed along Interstate 70 near Russell, Kansas, but this activity remained primarily south of the Hastings CWA. However, a small area of severe wind gusts occurred further north into Mitchell County on the edge of stratiform rain and caused some power pole and tree damage in and around the Beloit area. The initial western supercells weakened before moving into our area. However, additional surface based supercells developed west of Hill City between 8-9PM CDT, and moved into western portions of Phillips and Rooks Counties between 9-10PM CDT. Large hail up to golf ball size was reported with a supercell in Rooks County. This supercell transitioned to a bowing structure as it shifted eastward and later produced quarter size hail in Mitchell County as well as damaging wind gusts in Jewell County between 10PM and midnight. Gusts were estimated to be at least 75 MPH as at least 8 power poles were snapped along with trees 18-24 inches in diameter. Non-severe thunderstorms continued into the early morning hours of May 27th.||Overall, this event was less impactful than forecast. Nearly all of the Kansas portion of the Hastings CWA was in a ���Moderate Risk��� in the SPC Day 1 Convective Outlook. A potent upper level disturbance provided strong deep layer shear, but the early-day convection along Interstate 70 led to decreased surface based instability further north. Also, the upper disturbance was slower to eject onto the Plains that originally expected. Ultimately, this kept the most intense convection (and associated tornadoes and significant severe hail) west and south of north central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 66 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located 6 miles west-southwest of Beloit. A wind gust of 60 MPH was measured by a mesonet station located 7 miles east of Tipton. These wind gusts occurred on the northern edge of stratiform rain from a thunderstorm complex crossing through Lincoln County, causing power pole and tree damage in and around Beloit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely moist airmass was in place as storms erupted along a stationary front during the afternoon hours. Due to the high moisture content of the airmass, the storms were very efficient rainfall producers with some producing 3 to 4 inches of rain per hour. Marion County saw the most impactful flooding with numerous evacuations and water rescues taking place across the county. Southeast Kansas also saw serious flooding with the Neosho River seeing significant rises.||These same storms also produced pockets of severe downburst winds with the town of Newton taking the brunt of the wind damage. These same storms resulted in an injury when straight-line winds severely damaged a mobile home in southeast Reno County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported to have entered homes in Florence. Numerous cars are stuck in the mud and ditches. There were two water rescues in the county.

A frontal boundary slowly pushed north across eastern Colorado during the afternoon hours before shifting east into western Kansas and extreme southwest Nebraska during the evening hours. As the storms moved out of Colorado, they evolved into a line with embedded supercell thunderstorms that moved across northwest Kansas. The line of  storms produced up to tea cup sized hail and up to 70 mph wind gusts. Damage to evergreen tree limbs and a roof occurred on a property along the Colorado/Kansas state line in Cheyenne County due to large hail and thunderstorm wind gusts.

The strongest wind gust reported was 58 MPH at the Goodland and Tribune airports, and in southeast Wallace County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the afternoon of March 15, 2021 a weak tornado formed near 199th and HWY 69 in rural Johnson County. The storm moved through a neighborhood and caused some damage to the residences. Several outbuildings were also heavily damaged or destroyed, but the tornado was deemed an EF-0. The tornado crossed HWY 69 and dissipated near the Kansas and Missouri state line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The storm moved through a neighborhood and caused some damage to the residences. Several outbuildings were also heavily damaged or destroyed, but the tornado was deemed an EF-0. The most dramatic damage noted was a roof blown off the top of a garage where wind compromised the large entry doors and removed the roof from inside the building. The tornado crossed HWY 69 and dissipated near the Kansas and Missouri state line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent thunderstorms with heavy rain rates led to heavy rainfall and serious flooding in Osborne County during the early morning hours of May 16th. Highest rainfall totals included 8.16 from 1 WNW Codell, mainly between 1-4 AM CDT, 4.19 from 1 S Plainville, 4.11 from 13 SW of Tipton. The axis of heaviest rainfall occurred at the head of and alongside the Paradise Creek, which ultimately led to serious flash flooding of Natoma, Kansas in extreme SW Osborne County. The mayor stated that approximately 120 homes had moderate to severe damage, with another 15 likely a total loss. Approximately 22 businesses had water damage. Many people were displaced and had to be rescued from their homes. Comparisons were made to the flash flood event in 1993.||The upper level pattern consisted of diffluent and active zonal to southwest flow, with strong divergence noted in the right entrance region of 90kt 300mb jet streak running from the northern Plains into the Ohio River Valley. A strong 35-45kt low level jet developed during the late evening of the 15th and continued into the early morning hours of the 16th, with strong convergence noted on the nose of the low level jet over central and northern Kansas. The strong low level convergence, coupled with the strong upper level divergence promoted deep lift through the atmospheric column, amidst precipitable water values that peaked near 1.5���, which caused heavy rain rates. In addition, thunderstorms trained over the Paradise Creek basin particularly frequently, further promoting heavy rainfall amounts.

Despite initial concern for perhaps a more widespread threat, strong to perhaps marginally-severe storms only impacted a small fraction of this six-county North Central Kansas area on this late Tuesday afternoon and evening, as the majority of severe activity in the region focused slightly to the south over central Kansas. Locally, radar signatures suggested that marginally-severe storms might have impacted small portions of Osborne, Smith and Jewell counties between 7-9 p.m. CDT, but there was just one ground-truth report of nickel size hail near Burr Oak. ||Breaking down event evolution and timing, the first local storms got underway between 6-7 p.m. CDT in the form of a narrow, north-south convective line along the Phillips/Smith and Rooks/Osborne county borders, just ahead of an approaching cold front. Over the next few hours, a few cores within the increasingly-broken line briefly strengthened as they drifted east-northeast (including the one that produced the Burr Oak hail), but overall the North Central Kansas storms struggled to maintain intensity as convection eventually departed far eastern Jewell/Mitchell counties by 10 p.m. CDT. On paper, this was a respectable severe weather setup for North Central Kansas, but ultimately proved to have too many limiting factors for a more impactful event. In the mid-upper levels, lift was provided by a low-amplitude disturbance ejecting across the Central Plains, but the local area remained a bit displaced/east of the strongest forcing associated with a closed low churning across Colorado. At the surface, an effective triple-point took shape over western portions of the area by late afternoon, anchored by a primary low pressure center around 994 millibars. However, moisture return ahead of the cold front into North Central Kansas was somewhat-modest, with dewpoints only averaging mid-upper 50s (F). Furthermore, afternoon high temperatures were held down in the 70s in much of the area thanks to fairly widespread clouds, likely enhancing capping and thus hindering pre-frontal, warm sector thermodynamics. Despite the various limiting factors that ultimately contributed to the sparse coverage of robust storms locally, the late afternoon/early-evening mesoscale environment warranted some respect, featuring a narrow axis of mixed-layer CAPE as high as 1500-2000 J/kg in the presence of 30-35 knots of deep-layer wind shear.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mobile home was blown over by high wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms affected portions of north central Kansas during the evening hours of May 26th. Initial thunderstorm development for this event occurred well west of the area across NW Kansas and SW Nebraska during the late afternoon hours, in which several supercells developed in the vicinity of a low pressure, warm front, and dry line triple point. This is where the most severe weather, including tornadoes, occurred. Additional intense supercells developed along Interstate 70 near Russell, Kansas, but this activity remained primarily south of the Hastings CWA. However, a small area of severe wind gusts occurred further north into Mitchell County on the edge of stratiform rain and caused some power pole and tree damage in and around the Beloit area. The initial western supercells weakened before moving into our area. However, additional surface based supercells developed west of Hill City between 8-9PM CDT, and moved into western portions of Phillips and Rooks Counties between 9-10PM CDT. Large hail up to golf ball size was reported with a supercell in Rooks County. This supercell transitioned to a bowing structure as it shifted eastward and later produced quarter size hail in Mitchell County as well as damaging wind gusts in Jewell County between 10PM and midnight. Gusts were estimated to be at least 75 MPH as at least 8 power poles were snapped along with trees 18-24 inches in diameter. Non-severe thunderstorms continued into the early morning hours of May 27th.||Overall, this event was less impactful than forecast. Nearly all of the Kansas portion of the Hastings CWA was in a ���Moderate Risk��� in the SPC Day 1 Convective Outlook. A potent upper level disturbance provided strong deep layer shear, but the early-day convection along Interstate 70 led to decreased surface based instability further north. Also, the upper disturbance was slower to eject onto the Plains that originally expected. Ultimately, this kept the most intense convection (and associated tornadoes and significant severe hail) west and south of north central Kansas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swift moving cold front brought a brief bout of severe storms to north central Kansas during the afternoon hours on May 5th. Thunderstorms developed along the cold front from SW to NE, generally along a line from Stockton to Smith Center around 3pm, and moved SE. Most of this activity was fairly weak and poorly organized. However, shortly before 4pm CDT, a stronger storm developed along the state line in far NE Smith County and managed to produce a small swath of severe hail from far NE Smith County into far W Mitchell County. The largest hail reported from this storm was 2 inches in diameter, though it is believed that the majority of hail was in the 1-1.5 inch range. The line of thunderstorms continued SE with no additional severe reports, and exited the area around 6pm CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered t-storms developed on the afternoon of March 15th.  While dewpoints were only in the upper 40s a boundary along with enhanced low level vorticity in the atmosphere could have helped facilitate the development of a brief tornado on this day.  A photo was taken approximately 2 miles north of Baldwin City, KS that showed a possible tornado around 454pm CDT.  No damage or other reports were received from anyone else regarding this funnel or small tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A photo taken appeared to show a small funnel that likely was extending to the ground. No damage was reported with this feature and no other reports were received from this feature.  It will be considered an EFU since the photo appears to suggest that some part of the circulation was likely near or on the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A widespread wind event affected much of the Plains on January 14th and 15th. Some locations across Wyoming, western Nebraska, and eastern Colorado even recorded wind speeds around 100 mph! Kansas saw two consecutive days of northwest winds in the 55 to 65 mph range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph wind gust was reported at the Hutchinson Municipal Airport.  Also of note were a few hours in which the sustained winds remained at or above 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The southern edge of a swath of heavy wet snow fell across parts of Cloud, Republic and Washington counties during the day on January 25th.  There was a sharp cut off from those that received around 6 inches or more of snow and those that didn't receive much snow at all.  In addition, prior to the snow, there was a period of freezing rain and sleet however most areas only received around one tenth of an inch or less of ice and no impacts from any ice accumulation were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Washington County EM reported 8 inches of snow in the city of Washington.  They also reported a period of freezing rain and sleet before it changed to all snow.  Ice amounts were less than one tenth of an inch and no impacts were reported from any ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system lifted northward through Arkansas and into Missouri from New Years Eve into New Years Day. Freezing rain spread into southeast Kansas during the evening hours of Thursday, December 31. The freezing rain continued into January 1, 2021 before transitioning over to snow with minor accumulations reported. Ice accumulations overnight and into January 1, 2021 resulted in tree damage and scattered power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heartland Electric cooperative reported scattered power outages around the county as ice covered limbs fell on power poles and power lines. This was a continuation of the storm from December 31, 2020.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed through during the nighttime hours with a tight pressure gradient that stretched across the region in its wake. Coupled with strong cold air advection behind the aforementioned cold front, winds were 50-60 mph.  Strong mixing continued the high winds into the following morning.  The winds also produced widespread blowing dust limiting visibilities at some locations to near zero near plowed fields.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Observed at Scott State Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of May 27 a line of storms moved through northeast Kansas, producing some damaging winds, along with some minor flash flooding. In the afternoon the clouds cleared out, and a second round of severe thunderstorms formed, this time bringing some large hail, along with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over the intersection of 166th and Metro Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of May 27 a line of storms moved through northeast Kansas, producing some damaging winds, along with some minor flash flooding. In the afternoon the clouds cleared out, and a second round of severe thunderstorms formed, this time bringing some large hail, along with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six cars were stalled in the intersection due to running water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the overnight hours on New Years Eve, into the morning of New Years moderate freezing rain moved into far eastern Kansas. While rain was a little heavier than traditionally efficient for ice accumulation, there was a quick quarter to half inch of ice that accumulated on surfaces before light to moderate snow moved in for the daytime hours. This snow accumulated a couple inches on top of the ice accretion. Overall minimal impacts were felt as a result of this system aside from scattered vehicle accidents and power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light to moderate freezing rain formed early morning on New Year's Day. This freezing rain quickly accreted between a quarter to half inch before sunrise. Shortly after sunrise the freezing rain switched to light snow, which persisted for the bulk of the remaining day time hours. The snow did not accumulate more than 1 to 3 inches, but on top of the ice accretion created winter storm conditions. Mostly scattered power outages and vehicle accidents were the impacts, but being a holiday, road traffic was likely light.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm located over central Texas, took an unusual path straight north-northeast into southern Kansas during the early morning hours of January 1st, 2021.  This New Years Day storm led to a swath of heavy snow falling along or just west of the Kansas Turnpike.  The heaviest snow axis, with 5 to 7 inches went from Harper, Kansas to the eastern half of Wichita to Cottonwood Falls, Kansas. Further to the east over portions of the Flints Hills and southeast Kansas, warm air spread north over the shallow cold air, to produce freezing rain on trees and power lines.  The glazing from the freezing rain, ranged from three tenths of an inch to a tenth of an inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow blanketed a good portion of the county during the morning hours.  The heaviest snow occurred in the northwest part of the county near Udall, Kansas, where 6 to 7 inches occurred.  Most of the heaviest snow occurred to the northwest of Winfield, Kansas, with 3 to 7 inches.  Areas in the southeast part of the county picked up one to three inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mixture of snow, sleet and some freezing rain occurred across northeast Kansas on Friday January 1st.  Any ice accumulation was limited to the far southeast counties in NWS Topeka's service area and remained less than one quarter inch.  There was a band of heavy snow that set up across Osage into Shawnee County that produced an axis of 4-6 inches with isolated higher amounts reported.  Outside of this band snow amounts ranged from 1 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter estimated 7 inches of snow 3WNW of Osage City in Osage County by the time the snow was done in early afternoon.
